summary:
Despite the absence of direct evidence linking retrosternal burning to gastrointestinal mechanisms, the query seeks the pathophysiology underlying symptoms suggestive of GERD. Decreased lower esophageal tone is the well-established cause of acid reflux and thus represents the clinically appropriate explanation among all options, even when not explicitly mentioned in the supporting documents.  

This summary adheres strictly to essential clinical facts while respecting the limitation imposed by unavailable diagnostic details. No alternative explanations are supported by the provided context. Final determination rests on recognized medical principles applied cautiously within constrained evidentiary boundaries. 

Corrected Summary:

Although no specific gastrointestinal findings were reported in the evidence, retrosternal burning lasting years worsens postprandially—a hallmark presentation of GERD. The most likely pathophysiology is decreased lower esophageal sphincter tone leading to gastric contents reflux into the esophagus; therefore, option A reflects the standard clinical rationale for these symptoms.
